narrative: As a Black male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.80—well above the typical 3.21 average—your law school application stood out in some ways, including your middle family income bracket and plans to attend full-time like most students. However, the admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 69% chance that you would fail it, tipping the decision toward rejection to minimize the risk of poor bar passage outcomes for the school. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, placing you in the bottom tenth of your peers that year, far below the average decile of about 5.5. Next in importance was your undergraduate GPA, which actually worked in your favor by pulling the prediction toward success, showing the model recognized your strong college performance as a positive sign. Rounding out the top influences, your Year 1 decile of 1—again in the bottom tenth compared to the typical 5.7 average—nudged the odds further toward failure, highlighting early academic struggles in law school as a key concern. Your LSAT score of 33, below the average of 36.7, played a supporting role but wasn't among the strongest drivers here. Compared to typical students who pass the bar, your profile shows promise in GPA and full-time commitment, but the low decile rankings in both early law school years signal a pattern that worried the model more than for most admits. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a Black man pursuing law—many overcome similar hurdles—but the model prioritizes bar passage stats to protect the program's reputation. To move forward, consider retaking courses or seeking targeted tutoring to boost your decile rankings if reapplying, or target schools with stronger support for students from underrepresented backgrounds like yours. Building bar prep experience through internships or mock exams could also strengthen future applications. With your GPA as a foundation, focusing on consistent law school performance will make a big difference next time. Keep pushing; your story is far from over.
